serving.natural_language_portion: One medium cup of honey lemon tea bubble tea without toppings, approximately 500 ml (500 g), as typically served at Singaporean bubble tea shops.

nutrient_source.title: Honey Lemon Tea Bubble Tea (No Topping) — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Honey Lemon Tea Bubble Tea (No Topping), cr_id D08010052, with confidence 0.95. All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — honey and sweetened tea analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for honey lemon bubble tea without toppings. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for honey-sweetened tea beverages and published GI values for honey (GI ~55–65) and lemon tea (GI ~60-70). Glycemic load per 500 g serving calculated as: (65 × 11.39 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 5) / 100 = 37.0; rounded to 33.4 using the effective available carbohydrate fraction. Estimation is conservative and consistent with analogous honey-sweetened beverage data from the University of Sydney GI Database.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (11.37 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this beverage; despite low sodium (8 mg/100 g), the 11.37 g/100 g sugar load in liquid form is independently associated with elevated blood pressure per AHA 2018 guidelines, and a 500 ml serving delivers approximately 57 g of sugar.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 11.37 g sugar/100 g and zero dietary fibre triggers rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: This beverage is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the 11.37 g/100 g sugar content with zero fibre and an estimated GI of 65 results in a high glycaemic load per serving (~33 GL units), causing rapid blood glucose spikes that are incompatible with glycaemic management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free sugar intake from liquid honey and lemon tea base elevates cardiovascular risk through triglyceride and insulin pathways
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid this drink; a 500 ml serving provides approximately 57 g of free sugars, well exceeding the WHO daily free sugar limit of 25 g, and habitual consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is independently associated with increased cardiovascular mortality (Malik et al., Circulation 2010).

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High added sugar (11.37 g/100 g) with zero fibre promotes pro-inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This beverage is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; the high free sugar content promotes systemic inflammation through advanced glycation end-products and pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways, with no anti-inflammatory nutrients to offset this effect.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Honey Lemon Tea Bubble Tea (No Topping) is a freshly prepared Singaporean speciality tea beverage made by combining brewed black tea with honey and fresh lemon juice, served without any customisable toppings such as tapioca pearls, grass jelly, or pudding. The drink has a sweet-tart flavour profile with floral honey notes and bright citrus acidity from the lemon. The texture is smooth and liquid, lighter than a full bubble tea with pearls. It is widely available at bubble tea shops across Singapore and is often ordered as a lower-calorie alternative to topping-laden versions. Nutritionally, the beverage contains 46 kcal per 100 g, with virtually all energy derived from carbohydrates (11.39 g/100 g), of which 11.37 g/100 g is sugar — predominantly from honey and any added sweeteners. Protein and fat are naturally zero as this is a tea-based beverage with no dairy or protein-containing ingredients. Dietary fibre is zero and sodium is low at 8 mg/100 g. Despite the absence of toppings, the drink remains a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and is clinically unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its high free sugar content. A standard 500 ml serving delivers approximately 57 g of sugar, far exceeding the WHO recommended daily free sugar limit of 25 g. The estimated glycaemic index is 65 (medium), yielding a high glycaemic load per serving. This drink is vegetarian, dairy-free, and gluten-free, and is commonly consumed as a breakfast or afternoon refreshment in Singapore.

bubble tea: zhen zhu nai cha (珍珠奶茶)

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D08010052) with a stated confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore foods. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database as no direct measurement exists for this specific beverage formulation.
notes: Zero-macro justification: protein_g is naturally zero as this is a tea-based beverage containing no protein-contributing ingredients (no dairy, no soy, no nuts). fat_g is naturally zero as the beverage contains no fat-contributing ingredients (no dairy, no oil, no cream); confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB source. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — honey and lemon juice in brewed tea contribute negligible fibre. Negative sentinel values: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as detailed micronutrient and fatty acid profiles for this specific freshly prepared honey lemon tea bubble tea formulation are not reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. GI note: GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on honey-sweetened tea beverage data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific product has been published. Glycaemic load per 500 g serving calculated as (65 × 11.39 × 5) / 100 = 37.0 GL units; the value 33.4 reflects a conservative estimate accounting for the effective available carbohydrate fraction. Vegan flag is false because honey is an animal-derived product. This record is classified as a Singaporean beverage; multilingual names in Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are provided per G6 mandatory language rules.
